transcript.transcript: My name is Scott Kindler. Today is May 6, 1977. I'm going to interview Earl Keller, And the general topic will be the Coeur d'Alene area. Mr. Keller, when were you born? May 17, 1906. In Coeur d'Alene? In Coeur d'Alene. In fact, it's 616 Coeur d'Alene Avenue. And how long have you lived in Coeur d'Alene? I lived here continuously until 1934. And then for the next seven years, I spent in Glacier National Park. Was back periodically to visit my folks. And then I moved into Spokane and lived in Spokane until 1959. From there, I was in Moscow for a year or so, and then into Western Montana at Old Great Falls, Columbia Falls, Helena. When I was in Great Falls, I was a field engineer for a consulting engineering firm that had the contract to stake for the missile excavations when the missile program was going, the Minuteman missile program was going in the Great Falls. Where were your parents born? My dad was born in Manderville, Minnesota in 1869, July 5, 1869. Mother was born in Kansas. Oh, I can't think of the name of the town, but she was born on the 26th of June in 1871. My dad came west in a covered wagon train when he was about nine years old. They traveled through, they spent the first winter in the Dakotas. Now I don't know if it was North Dakota or South, because I'm not sure. And I'm rather, I'm sorry that I didn't ask him, because after I got into Montana, I wondered then if he, just how he crossed the state, if he crossed the state, if it was been, if they had traveled on the Oregon Trail, they would have had to have gone south because it didn't go through Montana at all. It cut through Wyoming and up, well Fort Hall, Idaho I think was on the Oregon Trail. They eventually wound up at Walla Walla and so it's possible that they that they may have taken the Mullin Trail. Because the Mullin Trail started at Fort Benton, Montana and went to Walla Walla, which was to connect the heads of navigation on the Missouri and the Columbia rivers. When did your parents move to the Coeur d'Alene area? I'm not real sure of that, although it was probably in the early 1900s. My dad had a, He and a man by the name of Charlie Denim or were partners in a grocery store I'm known as the V Keller and company and They went out of business through just offering too much credit. They wound up with somewhere around $8, 000 to $10, 000 in credit on the books. And in 1906, that was a hell of a lot of money. And I think that probably the store closed Shortly after I was born or in that in that era at least and he worked for other stores in town and Was it I traveled for a nursery outfit for a period of time All right When you were a small kid in Coeur d'Alene, what were the major differences as compared to the way Coeur d'Alene is now? Well, you remember the sleepy little town that I grew up in. We had, all the things were much, much quieter, less, there was no thought of crime. I don't know just, I don't remember specifically. But for example, I don't think we ever locked our door. And that's not recommended in these days by any means. And I can remember when 7th Street was paved. And at that time, the Mullen statue, which now is near the Mullen tree up at 4th of July Summit, was located at 7th and Sherman. And when Sherman was paved before Seventh, it had a paving that wore out quite badly. It was concrete. But they had rocks, a blue rock about the size of your fist or from there on smaller. And the paving consisted of spreading these rock and then laying a kind of a concrete mortar over the rock. And it wasn't too thick, because I can remember that the rock started wearing through that concrete. But that was, oh, I can't pinpoint it time-wise, but it was quite a long time ago. It was in, oh, I would suppose, probably that the paving on downtown must have been in 1912 or 1913, and as far as 7th is concerned. Or I suppose that might have been in the range of 17 or 18. I remember when it was done, I spent the night with the night watchman, stayed up all night with a night watchman on a paving project one time. So I'm sure I was probably 12 or 13 years old at least. You said your first job was as a railroad clerk. And where was the railroad station then? Well, I worked for the Spokane International Railroad, which is the station is still in existence down near the North Idaho College. It will soon be torn out, I expect, because I think the college has purchased that railroad right away. And obviously, I don't know whether the Station building will be torn down or moved or what will happen to it. But at that time that was in 1926 and I worked About three years for the year from 19 well until early 1930 and The Brought logs in from the on for the Ohio Match Company from Bird, Cammett, and Crick area. The logs came down on a standard gauge railroad that the Ohio match owned and joined the SI main line, the Spokane International Main Line at Corbin Junction, I believe. And then the local train, a Daily train brought them in and pushed all of the 20-some black cars with logs on. At that time there was a dock out into the lake. It was torn out, and during this low water you could see the the studs of the piling of that dock. They would be unloaded during the night and the empties would be hauled out again in the morning. Whatever happened to the, or do you remember the old electric railroad that ran out to Hayden Lake? Yes. It was, Well, I don't remember this particular instance, but I can remember my mother telling of it. There was in the early days of, oh, I would say probably in 1909, 1908 or 1909, I'd done two or three, maybe four years old, They were registering the land office for Homestead, and there was a large influx of people coming in to register for Homestead. And the electric line had, they were doing a tremendous business. Trains were packed. I don't know whether this was on a weekend or a Sunday or not, but anyhow, two of those trains came together, one coming in from Spokane and one going out. The accident, the wreck was down above, down at Gibbs, in the Gibbs area. And it was a horrible mess. I don't know how many were killed, but I'm sure many people were killed. And my mother took me in my baby buggy and went down. I'm sure not by that, not the day that it happened, but before the mess was cleaned up anyhow but that that train Cora you say went to Hayden Lake and but then a tavern was Built I think do for that purpose. Because in those days, the electric lines or the inner urban lines almost had to have some tourist attraction at the end of their line. The same thing was true of Natatorium Park in Spokane. It was at the end of the streetcar line and generated a lot of transportation travel. And the same thing was true of the Zana Tavern and the golf course. Of course, an ideal setting because Hayden Lake was a wonderful place for recreation. But the trains would go out there, and there was a, I don't think they had a why. I think it was a loop that they just made around the golf course, and so they could come back into town again without having to back up all that way. Okay. Do you remember the steamers on the lake? Oh, yes. You remember? There were many of them. Bonnie Doon and the Rambler, the Colfax. Gosh, I can't think of the others. Those were all smaller vessels. The Flyer and the Georgie Oaks were the large steamers of the Red Collar Line. Then there was another one, the Harrison, which was about the same size as the George Hills, maybe even a little bit bigger. I'm not positive of that. But it was based out of Harrison, principally, I think. It traveled to Coeur d'Alene. But the red collar line's home base was Coeur d'Alene. But I think the Harrison was based at Harrison. They both, I'm sure, went up to St. Joe as far as St. Mary's. What was their principal mean of business? Well, I think it varied. In the earlier days, there was no rail connection from Harrison Mountain to Spokane. And in the real early days, there was a small narrow-gauge railroad that was built from the old mission or Catalno area up in into the mining area and the ore would be put on put on this narrow-gauge train and brought down to the Coeur d'Alene River and transferred to barges, which were in turn hauled to Coeur d'Alene, down the Coeur d'Alene River and down the lake, and were loaded on to the NP, which had a branch line from Raftram into Coeur d'Alene and it would be a main line of the NP at Raftram. So that was the Ort travel. And that was, well, I can't pinpoint it. It was before my time, but I can remember hearing of it. I can remember my dad saying that occasionally—I don't know how frequently—the that occasionally, I don't know how frequently, the barges of lead ore would capsize. So there's lead ore on the bottom of the lake. And That, of course, was abandoned. That method was abandoned after the Union Pacific or the OWRN built their line to Harrison and on up to Wallace. Then, of course, it was possible to load their ore directly onto the train and avoid that transshipment fit. Were the Sunday, I know they brought Sunday excursion trains out from Spokane for people to ride on steamboats, but there's still a lot of that. Was that a big business? Oh, yeah. Mm-hmm. And the, yes, The electric line track extended out onto the dock a ways. And they would back their train out there for unloading and loading. And the steamer could pull up within, oh, 50 feet easily of the train, and the people would just get off the steamer and get on the train to go back in Spokane or wherever. Mostly, I think, the travel was from Spokane on those excursions. They would come in early in the morning and go up the lake, possibly as far as St. Mary's Isle, whether they could make that trip back again by nightfall or not, but they would – that was the excursion route at least. Did the steamboats tow barges? Oh, yes. Yeah, they towed barges. And of course, logging, the lumber industry, was another one of the steamboats' main sources of income, but not the larger vessels. The Harrison and the Flyer and the George Yokes hauled freight as well as passengers. Their lower deck was, in addition to the boiler space, engine space, they had quite a bit of space for loading freight, which would be possibly unloaded at various landings along the lake, as well as at Harrison and St. Mary's. What caused the demise of the steamers? Well, I suppose that automobile travel was probably as much to blame as anything. See, in those days there was no road around the lake. And the travel, the road to the Wallace Kellogg area wasn't all that good. Well, the present crossing at 4th of July Summit is the third one that I know of. I have traveled all three roads across there. The first one was up quite high. The second one was through the tunnel, which is still in existence and is used as a storage for sand, I believe now, and then the present four-lane highway. And I think that the road from Wolf Lodge on up to Harrison was probably a contributing factor that got rid of the steamers. Just lack of business is the reason, of course. So you went to Glacier Park. What did you do there while you were there? Well, the first year I was a levelman. I had two rod chainmen in my crew. And we supervised the construction of about $10, 000 worth of the masonry guardrail that was along the going to the Sun Highway on the west side of Logan Pass from there on down. In the second year, I worked for the Bureau of Public Roads. That was Bureau of Public Roads. In 1935, I spent some time with the Bureau of Public Roads. Then I worked for the Park Service off and on as an engineering foreman for the CCs. And prior to my appointment as an engineering foreman for the CC organization, I worked for the Hershey Conservation, worked as a temporary employee and using CC boys on a survey crew. I ran the line for what is a metallic transcontinental phone line that runs through Glacier Park. Prior to that time, they had used the normal forest service type of telephone, which is a single wire stretched on trees and posts. And it uses the ground to complete the circuit. But it isn't nearly as efficient as the metallic circuit. And so they ran a good full line across the park. Now, these CC men, These were men assigned to work there because of the Depression? Right. Civilian Conservation Corps was what it was. And there were men, but mostly they were boys of, oh, I suppose, 18, 17, 18, 19 years of age. I don't think anything less than that, younger than that. But a lot of the men that were elderly, or I say elderly, middle-aged, some middle-aged men, particularly from the western states and Montana more particularly. They were the leaders and were—and usually had a small crew of their own with a foreman over the squeeze. But the boys in two of the camps that I was in came from the sidewalks of New York, and they were—the first time they'd ever been off a pavement, and they weren't too sure whether they liked it or not. Well, with Glacier Park, how was it then compared to now? Well, I haven't been back there for a good many years. But it hasn't changed an awful lot. Of course, I'm sure that there are more campgrounds and more tourists. But there's still some back country there that you get at by saddle horse or backpacking. You mentioned that you worked as an engineer around the Coeur d'Alene area, did you? I worked for the Idaho Highway Department, starting in about 1939. And no, from about—see, I worked from about 1929 until 1934 for the highway department. Well, it wasn't steady work exactly, but mostly I was employed. There were some unemployment periods in that time on survey work and highway design and that sort of thing. What highways did you work on? Did you put any, construct any new highways? Did you work on any? I worked, my first job was working on that section of road out west of town that leads up to Micah, out towards the Micah Hill. And I worked on two sections in the Worley area, from Worley to Setters, and from Setters to what is known as Mud Bay School. I don't think the school is there anymore. But it was about in the range of a nine, ten mile stretch, all told. And then I worked on the Potterhorn Bay to Harrison section when they built in the present fill across the slough there, putting a new bridge over the Coeur d'Alene River. Previously there had been a bridge over the river, a wooden structure. Probably, I don't remember it too well, but across the slough was a wooden piling structure that was really a bridge about a mile long across there. Maybe not that long, but it was somewhat similar to the sand point, the bridge over the sand point. Were you associated with any of the... Was the lumbering industry that much different then than it is now? Oh, yes. I wasn't associated with lumber in spite of the fact that I was raised in a mill town. My only work in that industry at all was a job in the one sawmill when I was going to school at the box factory, which was located out in the northwest section of town. And I didn't work in the woods or in the mills at all. But things have changed tremendously, of course, in that there wasn't a truck on the road in those days. They didn't even know it was possible to haul by truck. There were no trucks to haul with. I think that there were wagons that they hauled logs with. But mostly it was skidding them to areas where they could be dumped into the streams. And when the high water came and were then floated down the tributary streams and the Seydoux River and on down and towed down the river and down the lake and log booms. And of course, the log booms are much different now than they were then. They were seen as log booms at that time. Now they're booms of bundles of logs. If you go out past the Potlatch and see that log boom there by the Intrepid, there's, there'll be bundles of ten or fifteen logs there. All right. Your parents when they came out here was there still Fort Sherman still In its form there down by the river? Well, I don't think so. I think it is probably, you see it was abandoned. Don't, it was established in 88 wasn't it? Something of that range. But The soldiers, during the Spanish-American War, the soldiers left the fort and went to form a part of our armed services in the Cuba in the Spanish-American War and just never came back to this location. It was, the fort was deactivated at that time. I think there was a token maintenance force there for a while, but about that time, I think it was when Fort George Wright in Spokane was established, and they just abandoned Fort Sherman. The buildings were still, I'm sure the buildings were completely intact when my parents were here. I can remember that the officer, what was known as officer's roll, some of those buildings are still in existence. But many of them have been either remodeled or torn down completely. And the commandant's residence was over facing the lake, and it had been torn down. I believe. I don't think it was moved anywhere. When was the dike constructed? It's around the present college. Well, it was there as long as I can remember. They had had problems with high water. And so it may not have been to its present elevation. But the previous to 1934, or 1933-34, which was our all-time high water in this area. The high water period before that was in 1894. And There was a sawmill, I think, down in the college area. I can remember there was a piling there along the riverbank that had a board nailed on it. And that was nailed at the elevation of the 1894 height of water. And in 33, 34, it exceeded that and completely flooded the park and the foreground area. But the dike, and I'm sure it went over that dike at that time too, probably. But that dike was there in its more or less present location and height long, for a long time they put the seawall You
